title: About
content: Shadi Mirmohammadi — Ph.D. candidate in Electrical & Computer Engineering at the University of Utah, working on terahertz/mmWave device design, EM simulation, and semiconductor thin-film fabrication. News Paper "Optically Tunable THz Metasurface Absorber Based on Fe-Doped β-Ga2O3" accepted at IRMMW-THz 2026, Salt Lake City. Oct 2026 School Selected as an in-person participant at the ASCENT Summer School on Semiconductor Science & Technology, University of Michigan. Aug 2026 Abstract "Excitation-Selective Eu³⁺ Luminescence and Defect Signatures in GdScO₃ Single-Crystal Substrates" submitted to MRS Fall 2026 (Symposium EL03: Wide and Ultra-Wide Bandgap Materials). Jun 2026 Milestone Joined the Electrical & Computer Engineering Ph.D. program at the University of Utah as a Graduate Research Assistant. Jan 2026 Poster Presented "Spin-Correlated Radical Pairs in Si QD–Organic Hybrids" at Pacifichem 2025, Honolulu. Dec 2025 Award Best Poster Award at Excited State Processes (ESP-2025), Santa Fe. Jun 2025 Talk Passed oral examination — "Harnessing Photoexcited Spin States in Silicon Quantum Dot–Organic Molecule Conjugates." Apr 2025 Award Awarded the College of Science EDGES Graduate Fellowship, University of Utah. 2023

title: Custom Instrumentation for Chiroptical Spectroscopy
content: A 3D-printed sample fixture for the CD spectrometer, designed in Fusion 360 — and the chiral metasurface signal it made reproducible. Shadi Mirmohammadi — Tang Lab, University of UtahThe problem: a measurement that moves when the sample doesCircular dichroism (CD) measures the difference in absorption between left- and right-circularly polarized light — for a chiral metasurface, a signal of a few millidegrees riding on a much larger absorbance. At that scale, where the substrate sits in the beam matters: hand-placing a coated fused-silica chip in the spectrometer’s sample compartment shifts and reshapes the spectrum from scan to scan, making successive measurements of the same sample hard to compare.The fix: design the sample’s seatSo I designed the seat. Two parts, modeled in Fusion 360 and 3D-printed: a rail mount that slides into the Jasco spectrometer’s sample compartment at a fixed position along the beam path, and a square substrate holder — a recessed pocket with corner mounting holes that seats the chip at the same position and orientation every time. The holder took six design iterations to get right. The two Fusion 360 parts (CAD preview renders): (a) the rail mount that fixes the position along the beam path; (b) the square substrate holder, v6 — a recessed pocket and mounting holes that make sample placement repeatable.The payoff: a clean bisignate signal — and proof the fixture mattersWith the fixture in place, the diarylethene (DAE) chiral metasurface shows a clear bisignate CD couplet across ~580–615 nm: a negative lobe reaching −10.5 mdeg near 580 nm and ...

title: Electrochemical Sol-Gelation of CdS Quantum Dots
content: Growing 3D quantum-dot gel networks with a voltage knob — phase- and size-dependent electrogelation of CdS QDs. Gels. Grown with volts. Watch zinc-blende CdS quantum dots assemble themselves into a three-dimensional network — live at the electrode, at 1.8 V. Your browser does not support the video tag. 1.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl · 36 µM CdS@TGA in methanol · gel nucleating on the Pt anodeShadi Mirmohammadi — Materials Chemistry, University of UtahThe idea: a voltage knob for building solids out of quantum dotsColloidal quantum dots are exquisitely tunable — their band gap and color follow their size — but as isolated particles wrapped in insulating ligands, they are hard to wire into anything macroscopic. Gelation solves this: cross-link the dots into a continuous, porous 3D network that keeps quantum confinement while allowing electronic communication, with enormous surface area for catalysis and sensing.Most QD gels are made by dumping in a chemical oxidant — simple, but it leaves residue and nucleates uncontrollably. This project uses electrochemistry instead: anodic oxidation of surface thiolates forms S₂²⁻ bridges between particles, so the gel grows directly on the electrode. The applied potential becomes a clean, tunable handle on oxidation strength — controlled kinetics, no chemical oxidant, and reversible, redox-active inter-particle bonds.Making the building blocksZinc-blende CdS quantum dots are synthesized by hot injection, with size dialed in by the growth hold time and sulfur precursor amount — batches from 2.6 to 4.9 nm with narrow spectral linewidths (HWHM ≈ 1...

title: Photon Upconversion in CdSe–Anthracene Hybrids
content: Turning two green photons into one violet photon with nanoplatelet–molecule hybrids — and why the geometry of a single carboxylic acid decides whether the energy flows. Seyedeh Shadi Mirmohammadi, Farwa Awan, Tsumugi Miyashita, Todd D. Krauss, and Ming Lee TangUniversity of Utah & University of RochesterPoster presented at the 2nd International Symposium on Photochemistry & Functional Interfaces (ISPF2), New York, 2024 —view the poster (PDF). Green in, blue out — on the bench. The 532 nm beam floods the table green, but the cuvette itself glows blue: anti-Stokes emission from triplet–triplet annihilation, light climbing uphill in energy.Making light climb uphillPhoton upconversion fuses two low-energy photons into one higher-energy photon — green in, violet out. That lets a solar cell or photocatalyst harvest photons that would otherwise pass straight through, and gives bioimaging deep-penetrating excitation with visible readout. The workhorse mechanism is triplet–triplet annihilation (TTA), and it takes a three-part relay: a light absorber — here a CdSe nanoplatelet — soaks up 532 nm (2.34 eV) photons; a mediator covalently bound to the nanoplatelet surface accepts the triplet by Dexter transfer (TET1); a free emitter in solution — 9,10-diphenylanthracene (DPA) — collects the triplet (TET2), and when two DPA triplets meet, TTA launches one of them to the singlet, which emits at 410 nm (3.06 eV). The relay: green photons absorbed by the CdSe nanoplatelet (2.34 eV) hop as triplets through the surface-bound anthracenecarboxylic acid (T₁ = 1.83 eV) to DPA (T₁ = 1.77...

title: EIT-Like Terahertz Metasurface — a Full-Wave Design Study
content: Reproducing and extending a published EIT terahertz metasurface in Ansys HFSS — skin-depth-aware metal thickness, substrate selection, and coupled-resonator physics extracted from full-wave data. Shadi Mirmohammadi — ECE 6310 (Advanced Electromagnetic Fields), University of Utah, Spring 2026 —read the report (PDF)Making a metasurface transparent — on purposeElectromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) is a quantum-optics effect with a metamaterial twin: couple two bright resonators so their responses destructively interfere, and a narrow transparency window opens between two absorption dips — along with the steep phase dispersion that makes slow light and ultrasensitive sensing possible. Zhang et al. (Optics Express, 2022) built one at terahertz frequencies: an aluminum C-arc + S-arc pair on polyimide, with a transparency window at 0.87 THz and a peak group delay of 5.5 ps.This project rebuilds that metasurface from scratch in Ansys HFSS — and then asks two fabrication questions the original paper never did.The questions the paper didn’t ask How thick does the metal actually need to be? At 1 THz, aluminum’s skin depth is δs ≈ 84 nm — yet the paper deposits 200 nm, 2.4× more than needed for opacity. Since resonance quality saturates once the film exceeds δs, thinner films may work identically while cutting deposition time and film stress. Is polyimide the right substrate? Its THz loss tangent (0.05) is among the worst of the available thin-film polymers — Parylene-C and BCB are 10–17× lower loss at similar permittivity, which should directly narrow the EIT window and st...

title: 10 kV β-Ga₂O₃ Photoconductive Switches
content: Switching 10 kV with a near-infrared laser — impurity-engineered Fe:β-Ga₂O₃ photoconductive semiconductor switches. Hunter Ellis, Imteaz Rahaman, Afrouz Taherian, Seyedeh Shadi Mirmohammadi, Botong Li, Michael A. Scarpulla, Berardi Sensale-Rodriguez, and Kai FuDepartment of Electrical & Computer Engineering, University of UtahManuscript ready for submission (2026).The idea: switching 10,000 volts with a laser pointer’s wavelengthPhotoconductive semiconductor switches (PCSSs) are optically triggered high-voltage switches — compact, fast, and with almost no off-state leakage — of interest for pulsed-power systems, ultrawideband radar, and grid-scale power electronics. Ultrawide-bandgap (UWBG) semiconductors like β-Ga₂O₃ (Eg ≈ 4.8 eV) are ideal hosts because blocking voltage scales with critical field. But there’s a catch: switching a UWBG material with band-to-band absorption requires deep-ultraviolet light, and high-power, fast UV sources are scarce.The workaround is impurity engineering. Fe doping introduces deep acceptor states ~0.8 eV below the conduction band of β-Ga₂O₃, so an ordinary 808 nm near-infrared laser can photoionize electrons from the Fe states into the conduction band — sub-bandgap optical triggering that keeps the material’s full high-voltage blocking capability. (It’s the same Fe:β-Ga₂O₃ photoconductivity that powers my optically tunable THz metasurface — one material platform, two very different devices.) Transition-metal impurity levels inside the 4.8 eV gap of β-Ga₂O₃. Shallow levels like Fe (0.8 eV below the conduction band) are reachable with n...

title: Excitation-Switchable Eu³⁺ Luminescence in GdScO₃
content: A nominally undoped crystal that changes its emission color with the excitation wavelength — and what its glow reveals about substrate quality. Seyedeh Shadi Mirmohammadi, Afrouz Taherian, Yunshan Wang, Diego P. Fernandez, Michael A. Scarpulla, Steve Blair, and Berardi Sensale-RodriguezUniversity of UtahManuscript ready for submission to Optical Materials Express (2026).A crystal that isn’t supposed to glowGdScO₃ is a workhorse perovskite substrate for growing epitaxial oxide films — prized for its lattice match to titanates, not for its optics. The substrates we studied are commercial, nominally undoped single crystals from two different vendors (Stonylab (100) and CrysTec (110)). Shine deep-UV light on them, though, and they light up with bright, sharp visible emission.The culprit turned out to be europium at the parts-per-million level — an unintentional trace impurity, thousands of times below deliberate doping, roughly one Eu³⁺ ion per 10⁵–10⁶ Gd³⁺. The reason such a vanishing trace glows so brightly is the host itself: the crystal is a dense lattice of optically active Gd³⁺, and energy absorbed anywhere in the Gd³⁺ sublattice migrates to the rare Eu³⁺ sites. The whole crystal acts as an antenna for its own impurities. The GdScO₃ perovskite structure: ScO₆ octahedra (blue) with Gd³⁺ (purple) between them. A trace Eu³⁺ ion (gold) substitutes on a Gd site — one per 10⁵–10⁶ Gd — and the Gd³⁺ sublattice funnels absorbed UV energy to it.The switch: 13 nm of tuning flips the emission colorUsing the same tunable deep-UV source I built for my GeO₂ photodetector measurements...

title: UV Photoresponsivity of GeO₂ Photodetectors
content: Measuring the deep-UV spectral response of ultrawide-bandgap GeO₂ detectors with a tunable third-harmonic laser source — a collaboration with the Fu and Blair labs. Shadi Mirmohammadi — with Imteaz Rahaman and Botong Li (Dr. Kai Fu’s lab) and Prof. Steve Blair, Electrical & Computer Engineering, University of UtahThe questionGermanium dioxide (GeO₂) is an emerging ultrawide-bandgap semiconductor: the film used here has a bandgap of ~4.7 eV, corresponding to an absorption edge near 264 nm — deep in the UV, where sunlight doesn’t reach the ground. That makes it a natural candidate for solar-blind UV photodetectors. Dr. Kai Fu’s group fabricated metal–semiconductor–metal detectors on their GeO₂ films (interdigitated contacts, 250 × 300 µm active area) and confirmed they respond to a UV flashlight. The real test, though, is quantitative: how does the responsivity vary with wavelength across the band edge?The trick: making deep-UV light at any wavelength you wantTunable light at 230–280 nm doesn’t come out of a lamp. We generated it by third-harmonic generation (THG) of a tunable Ti:sapphire laser in Prof. Blair’s lab — my setup for UV-pumped photoluminescence, realigned for this experiment. Tuning the fundamental sweeps the third harmonic across ~233–280 nm, with the power at each wavelength calibrated on a Thorlabs power meter with a UV extension.At each wavelength we recorded dual-sweep I–V curves from −10 to +10 V in the dark and under illumination — waiting for the photocurrent to stabilize before each sweep, and logging laser power on the same schedule — so that res...

title: Engineering Spin States in Si QD–Organic Hybrids
content: Can chemistry program a quantum dot's spin? Time-resolved EPR reveals that Si quantum dots hand their anthracene partners a fully spin-polarized triplet. Mandefro Y. Teferi, Sanoop Mambully Somasundaran, Shadi Mirmohammadi, Ming Lee Tang, Jens Niklas, and Oleg G. PoluektovArgonne National Laboratory & Department of Chemistry, University of UtahSubmitted to the Journal of the American Chemical Society (2026).The question optical spectroscopy can’t answerQuantum dot–molecule hybrids are one of the most promising platforms for harvesting long-lived triplet excitons — the workhorses of photon upconversion, photocatalysis, and singlet fission. Silicon QDs are especially attractive: nontoxic, earth-abundant, and covalently functionalizable. But nearly everything known about triplet energy transfer (TET) in Si QD–molecule conjugates comes from optical measurements — photoluminescence and transient absorption — and light cannot see spin. Whether a triplet formed by ordinary spin–orbit intersystem crossing, by charge recombination, or by direct energy transfer is invisible to a photodetector.Time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance (TR-EPR) can tell the difference. This work is the first TR-EPR study of triplet energy transfer in Si QD–organic hybrids.The system: three molecules, three linkersThe conjugates pair 2.65 nm dodecane-capped Si QDs with three anthracene derivatives, covalently bound through different bridges (~7 acceptors per QD): 9-ethylanthracene (9EA) through a flexible single bond, 9-vinylanthracene (9VA) through a π-conjugated C=C linker, and 9,10-bis(phe...

title: Optically Tunable THz Metasurface Absorber
content: An optically reconfigurable terahertz metasurface on Fe-doped β-Ga₂O₃ — IRMMW-THz 2026 (accepted). Seyedeh Shadi Mirmohammadi and Berardi Sensale-RodriguezDepartment of Electrical & Computer Engineering, University of UtahAccepted at the 51st International Conference on Infrared, Millimeter, and Terahertz Waves (IRMMW-THz 2026), Salt Lake City, October 2026.AbstractWe present an optically tunable terahertz (THz) metasurface absorber based on Fe-doped β-Ga2O3, an ultrawide-bandgap semiconductor (Eg ≈ 4.8 eV). Despite this large bandgap, Fe acceptor states enable sub-bandgap photoexcitation at 808 nm, producing pump-power-dependent photoconductivity. Full-wave electromagnetic simulations for σ = 0 to 25 S/m show 82.2% absorption modulation and 95.0% reflected-power modulation at 300 GHz, establishing Fe:β-Ga2O3 as a promising platform for optically reconfigurable THz photonics.Introductionβ-Ga2O3 is an ultrawide-bandgap semiconductor of interest for power electronics and UV photonics. In Fe-doped β-Ga2O3, mid-gap Fe acceptor states lie approximately 0.85 eV below the conduction band. Under sub-bandgap 808 nm continuous-wave illumination, electrons are excited from these acceptors into the conduction band, producing THz photoconductivity that increases with pump power [1]. Jiang et al. reported photoinduced conductivities up to ~25 S/m at 2 W pump power [1]. This response motivates THz metasurfaces that use photoinduced conductivity for contact-free tuning without electrical contacts, gates, or temperature control.Device DesignThe device consists of a gold H-shaped spli...
